title: Calling on Federal law enforcement, led by the Federal Bureau of Investigation, the Department of Homeland Security, and the Federal Aviation Administration, to provide an immediate briefing to the public regarding the recent drone activity in New Jersey and New York.
plain_summary: This bill asks the FBI and other federal agencies to tell the public what they know about mysterious drones that have been seen flying over New Jersey and New York. People have been reporting these drone sightings since late 2024 and are worried about what they might be. The House of Representatives wants these government agencies to hold a public meeting right away to explain what is happening. They also want federal and local police to work together to stop any dangerous or illegal drone activity that could hurt people.
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Aviation.

sections.title: Congressional Resolution 118
sections.text: This is a resolution from the House of Representatives asking federal law enforcement agencies to tell the public what they know about recent drone sightings in New Jersey and New York. The resolution was submitted by Representative Gottheimer on February sixth, twenty twenty five. It was sent to three committees in Congress for review: the Judiciary Committee, the Transportation and Infrastructure Committee, and the Homeland Security Committee.

sections.title: Background Facts About the Drone Activity
sections.text: Starting in the weeks before Thanksgiving twenty twenty four, people living in New Jersey began seeing unknown drones flying in the sky. People in at least ten counties across New Jersey reported seeing unmanned aircraft systems (drones that fly without a pilot inside) and other drone-like objects. Some witnesses said certain drones were as big as small cars or planes. On December eighth, twenty twenty four, there were forty nine reported drone sightings in just one night in New Jersey. As of September twenty twenty, the Federal Aviation Administration had registered about one point seven million unmanned aircraft systems. More than seventy percent of these are used for fun and hobbies. While drones and new technology help businesses and the economy, they can also be used for bad purposes, including criminal activities and terrorist attacks. On November twenty sixth, twenty twenty four, drones flying near a medical helicopter landing area stopped the helicopter from helping a car crash victim in Somerset County, New Jersey. Drones have been seen near important places like Picatinny Arsenal (a military facility) and Round Valley Reservoir, which provides water to New Jersey. On December thirteenth, twenty twenty four, Stewart Airport in Orange County, New York had to close its runways for about one hour because of drone activity in the airspace. The airport is run by the Port Authority of New York and New Jersey. Drones and other unidentified flying objects have also been reported in several other states besides New Jersey and New York. On January twenty eighth, twenty twenty five, White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt said that many of these drones were authorized and known to the government. She also said that after research and study, the drones flying over New Jersey in large numbers were authorized to be flown by the Federal Aviation Administration for research and various other reasons.

sections.title: What the House of Representatives is Asking For
sections.text: The House of Representatives recognizes the hard work of state and local police in dealing with public concerns about drone activity. The House calls on three federal agencies to be more open and share information with the public about recent drone activity when possible. These agencies are the Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI), the Department of Homeland Security, and the Federal Aviation Administration. The House urges these three agencies to hold an immediate public briefing to inform people about the current drone situation in New Jersey. The House calls on federal law enforcement agencies, led by the FBI and Department of Homeland Security, to work together with state and local police to use resources that can stop unauthorized drone activity. The House requests that the FBI, Department of Homeland Security, and Federal Aviation Administration review whether additional security measures are needed to protect important infrastructure and public safety from possible drone-related threats.
